Project X is our internal name, for now, for the example business we'll create following the framework we've developed for the durable business. We'll turn the idea into a business that will grow from $0, where we are now, to $100,000 plus in revenue, and you'll see what we do every step of the way. We've started this project with nothing, no list, no website, no advertising accounts, no audience, no offer, and, most importantly, no idea. And, to be fair, we're going to create this business outside the internet marketing space, so we don't have any implicit advantages from our reputations and experience. We've even considered changing our names for Project X, Tweetle Dumb and Tweetle D, Batman and Robin, or just Bob and Bill. Finally, we have one more, significant, challenge, instead of one of us going through the discover process to identify interests, experiences, and skills, we're going through the process together, collaboratively. That adds an interesting layer of complexity. Don't try this at home, folks. We never said this would be easy. We seem to have a high-paying threshold for seeking out discomfort, which is likely not normal. We should probably see a professional about this at some point. As a refresher, these are the questions we answered, reworded as first-person plural, from Discover, Part 1. What interests us? What skills have we learned? What hobbies have we pursued? What experiences have we had? How do we choose to spend our free time? What consumes a large part of our mind space? What would our calendar and credit card statements say about our interests? Example, our Amazon book Buying Habits reveals a lot about us, 28 book purchases so far in January by Andre for fiction and nonfiction, and 12 nonfiction book purchases by Sean. How do we spend our time, attention, and discretionary money? The answers to those questions were captured on three lists. Our skills. Our hobbies slash interests. Our experience, professional and personal. We have many shared interests, skills, and experiences. We're fortunate to share deeply held values too. When we put all of that together, we kept coming back to a central theme, creativity. But not just creativity in general, that's way too vague. Specifically, we're fascinated by the creative process, the occasionally frustrating and often magical experience of turning raw materials into new ways of thinking about and understanding the world around us, and sharing those ideas in our work and with our audiences. When we asked why repeatedly at every turn, we eventually revealed a core driver that resonates deeply for both of us. What is it that we do intuitively and subconsciously to guarantee a steady flow of new, high quality ideas to learn, understand, implement, and teach? The subconsciously part is an interesting component because it shines a light on mechanisms that are just outside of our conscious awareness and, very likely, our understanding of why a behavior works for us. Both of us are infinitely curious, so no doubt this alone is going to pull us to want to discover and learn more about what we don't already know and yet understand. Do is the most important word in that sentence. Our careers and livelihoods depend on our ability to generate and execute interesting and unique ideas consistently, day after day, year after year. By Stephen Pressfield's definition, we are professionals, meaning we show up for work knowing that we'll find our inspiration when we arrive, as opposed to his definition of amateurs who wait for inspiration to arrive before they show up to work. After identifying that starting place, we immediately realized how easy it is to jump to validation. Ideas for solutions and offers emerged almost immediately, a pulling force we need to wrestle with and push back on until later in the process. However, the discover process begins with our skills, interests, and experiences. Before we move on to potential solutions, we need to find audiences who could benefit from what we already know. We started our audience research by identifying books about creativity that we found most valuable, including The Breakout Principal, Herbert Benson, M.D. and William Proctor, The War of Art, Stephen Pressfield, A Technique for Producing Ideas, James Webb Young, The MacGyver Secret, Lee David Slotoff, The Inner Game of Work, Timothy Galway, Big Magic, Creative Living Beyond Fear, Elizabeth Gilbert, Creativity, Flow and the Psychology of Discovery and Invention, Mihai Cheeks and Mihai. Discipline Dreaming, A Proven System to Drive Breakthrough Creativity, Josh Linkner. Once we had our initial list, we scanned Amazon reviews looking for anything that captured our attention. What did reviewers complain about? How did they describe who they thought the ideal audience would be for the books? What did they think was really good? What did reviewers say was missing? We captured, and will continue to capture, those excerpts in a Google Doc named Project X Audience Research. That document will expand and evolve over time. Reviewers will mention other books will add to the list, and will read those reviews for insights as well. Once we reached critical mass with our audience research, we began to notice themes emerge. Initially, we captured those themes in another document in a list of anything interesting we noticed. Over time we organized that list by jobs, pains, and gains. We named that document Project X, themes from audience research. Both of those are living documents, they'll likely be different every time you look at them. Your documents don't need to look the same, but they should contain similar information. Your sources may be different, for example, Facebook group comments instead of Amazon reviews, but the general idea is the same. We're capturing the language of our potential audience to better understand what they hope to gain, avoid losing, and want to accomplish. We don't know, yet, who our initial minimum viable audience will be. People interested in creativity is way too broad. However, we believe that our MBA exists within that larger audience, and we're looking for clues to find them. There is a delicate balance between enough research to stack probabilities in our favor and too much research that turns into naval gazing procrastination. There isn't a uniform answer to know where the balance is, so use your best judgment. We've started to see patterns emerge, and we're beginning to develop some ideas for an MBA. However, there's more research to do. Our next steps are finishing mining book reviews for potential jobs, pains, gains, and other insights. Pulling on any interesting threads in reviews to other books, we should consider and mine those reviews as well. Narrow down our large audience to a smaller subset based on a broad collection of the jobs, pains, and gains that interest us most. Find Facebook and LinkedIn groups for that smaller audience and research their posts, articles, and comments. Update our audience research and themes documents. Reach out to potential prospects for one-to-one interviews, zoom and email. Continue to compile our research until we feel like we're ready to clearly define our initial MBA. We'll describe our process and results in the forthcoming Project X, Part 2. Andre and Sean
